---
title: "Your Paper Title"
subtitle: "Preliminary draft --- please do not cite without permission"
author:
  - name: Your Name
    email: you@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Your Department, Your University
        address: Some Address, Country
        corresponding: true
  - name: Co-Author One
    email: one@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Co-Author Department, Co-Author University
        address: Some Address, Country
  - name: Co-Author Two
    email: two@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Co-Author Department, Co-Author University
        address: Some Address, Country
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  A short abstract goes here. Summarize the paper's question, data,
  method, and main findings in 150--250 words. Replace this text with
  your own.

  \bigskip
  \noindent\textbf{Keywords:} keyword one, keyword two, keyword three

  \noindent\textbf{JEL Classification:} A00, B00, C00
keywords:
  - keyword one
  - keyword two
  - keyword three
thanks: |
  We thank seminar and conference participants for helpful comments.
  Replace this acknowledgments block with your own.
bibliography: references.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike
    keep-tex: true
    # Skip Pandoc's \paragraph redefinition, which clashes with the
    # titlesec/sectsty section-formatting we apply below.
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % Quarto's default template already loads amsmath, amssymb,
        % booktabs, graphicx, caption, and hyperref. The blocks below
        % add the structural conventions of a typical econ / finance
        % working paper: italic subsection headings, period-after-
        % section numbering, one-and-a-half spacing in tables, a
        % small breathing space around equations, an indented quote
        % env, theorem environments, and proper \begin{appendices}
        % support for an in-paper appendix.

        \usepackage{amsthm}
        \usepackage{subcaption}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{tabularx}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{sectsty}

        % One-and-a-half spacing inside tables (body is doublespaced
        % via the linestretch YAML setting).
        \AtBeginEnvironment{tabular}{\onehalfspacing}

        % Italic subsection / subsubsection headings (sectsty avoids
        % the titlesec / Pandoc \paragraph compatibility problem).
        \subsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \subsubsectionfont{\large\itshape}

        % Period after section number — headings read "1. Introduction"
        % instead of "1 Introduction".
        \makeatletter
        \def\@seccntformat#1{\@ifundefined{#1@cntformat}%
            {\csname the#1\endcsname\quad}%
            {\csname #1@cntformat\endcsname}%
        }
        \def\section@cntformat{\thesection.\quad}
        \def\subsection@cntformat{\thesubsection \quad}
        \makeatother

        % Long-URL tolerance.
        \tolerance=1
        \emergencystretch=\maxdimen
        \hyphenpenalty=10000
        \hbadness=10000

        % Quote environment indented 1.5cm on each side.
        \renewenvironment{quote}
          {\normalsize\list{}{\rightmargin=1.5cm \leftmargin=1.5cm}%
           \item\relax}
          {\endlist}

        % Equation environment with small vspace after the display.
        \let\oldequation=\equation
        \let\endoldequation=\endequation
        \renewenvironment{equation}{%
          \vspace{0cm}\begin{oldequation}}{\vspace{0.25cm}\end{oldequation}%
        }

        % Theorem-style environments.
        \theoremstyle{plain}
        \newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}[section]
        \newtheorem{proposition}[theorem]{Proposition}
        \newtheorem{lemma}[theorem]{Lemma}
        \theoremstyle{definition}
        \newtheorem{definition}[theorem]{Definition}
        \newtheorem{assumption}[theorem]{Assumption}
---

```{=tex}
\vspace{-0.50cm}
```

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

State the research question, motivate why it matters, summarize the
contribution, and preview the main findings. Close with a
one-paragraph roadmap of the remaining sections.

# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

Set up the model. State primitives, the agent's problem, and the
equilibrium concept. Translate the model's predictions into
hypotheses tested in the empirical sections.

## Testable Predictions {#sec-theory-predictions}

Spell out the predictions that follow from the model. Each prediction
should be tight enough that the data can support or reject it.

# Data {#sec-data}

Describe the sample, sources, and any cleaning. Full variable
definitions appear in @sec-app-vardesc.

## Data Sources and Sample Construction {#sec-data-sources}

List each data source, the time window, and the merge / filter rules.

## Variable Definitions {#sec-data-vars}

Define the dependent variable, the focal regressor, and any controls.

## Summary Statistics {#sec-data-summary}

Discuss summary statistics. Reference @tbl-summary for the numerical
table.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics."
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
# knitr::kable(your_data_frame, booktabs = TRUE)
```

# Methodology {#sec-method}

State the estimator and identifying assumptions. A representative
baseline specification:

$$
y_{it} = \alpha_i + \beta\, x_{it} + \gamma' \mathbf{Z}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$ {#eq-baseline}

where $y_{it}$ is the outcome of interest, $x_{it}$ the focal
regressor, and $\mathbf{Z}_{it}$ a vector of controls. Identification
of $\beta$ rests on the assumptions discussed below.

::: {.assumption}
State the key identifying assumption here.
:::

# Main Results {#sec-results}

Lead with the headline result. Discuss the magnitude and what it
means in context.

## Baseline Estimates {#sec-results-baseline}

Present the baseline estimate of @eq-baseline and discuss it.

## Heterogeneity {#sec-results-hetero}

Document how the baseline effect varies along observable dimensions.

## Mechanisms {#sec-results-mechanism}

Decompose the headline effect into the channel(s) the theory predicts.

# Additional Analyses {#sec-additional}

Present any extension that does not fit the main narrative but is
useful context for readers.

# Robustness {#sec-robust}

Re-estimate the baseline under alternative specifications, placebo
samples, and falsification tests. Detailed robustness tables that do
not fit in the main text live in the Internet Appendix.

# Conclusion {#sec-conc}

Summarize the contribution, acknowledge limitations, and suggest
directions for follow-up work.

```{=tex}
\newpage
% The \appendix macro is built into the article class: it resets the
% section counter, switches \thesection to A, B, C, ..., and continues
% numbering tables/figures/equations within each appendix section.
\appendix
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
\renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{section}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{figure}}
\renewcommand{\theequation}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{equation}}
```

# Variable Definitions {#sec-app-vardesc}

Provide formal definitions and data sources for every variable used
in the empirical analysis. A table works well here.

# Auxiliary Derivations {#sec-app-derivations}

Walk through derivations or proofs sketched in the main text.

::: {.proof}
Sketch the argument or refer to the Internet Appendix for the full
proof.
:::
